mnemonics: Dense = 'D' for 'Dots Everywhere': In a dense set, around every point in the space, you can find a dot (point) from the dense set, no matter how small the neighborhood.
keyPoints: A subset D of a metric space (X,d) is dense if its closure is the whole space: Cl(D) = X.
A metric space is separable if it contains a countable dense subset.
The rational numbers Q are a countable dense subset of the real numbers R, making R a separable metric space.
commonMistakes: Confusing a dense set with the whole space itself. A dense set can be much smaller (e.g., Q is dense in R, but R also contains irrationals not in Q).
Assuming a space is not separable just because it seems 'large'. For example, the space of continuous functions on [0,1] with the supremum norm is separable, despite being infinite-dimensional.
